name: Pipeline Parallelism

x.coordination: Work is split into ordered stages connected by queues; each stage runs concurrently on different items, like an assembly line. Throughput is set by the slowest stage.
hazards: Slowest stage bottlenecks the whole pipeline
Bounded queues need backpressure; unbounded ones leak memory

def: A parallel pattern that divides processing into a sequence of stages, each running concurrently on a stream of items, so different items occupy different stages at once.
problem: A long sequential transformation can overlap in time when many items flow through it — increasing throughput without parallelising a single item.
mechanism: Stages are connected by (usually bounded) queues; stage k processes item i while stage k−1 processes item i+1. Steady-state throughput equals 1/(slowest stage time); balancing and backpressure keep it efficient.
benefits: Overlaps heterogeneous work for high throughput
Maps to CPU cores, GPU streams, or services
limits: Throughput bounded by the slowest stage
failures: Unbounded queues → memory blow-up under load
whenNot: A single item must be sped up (use data parallelism)
myths: "Pipelining lowers latency" — it raises throughput; latency may rise
